frameworks.name: task-relevant autoencoder via classifier enhancement (TRACE)
frameworks.one_line: autoencoder jointly trained with classifier to isolate behaviorally-relevant neural features

frameworks.name: standard autoencoder
frameworks.one_line: unsupervised reconstruction baseline that may capture noise rather than behavior

frameworks.name: machine learning for fMRI representation discovery
frameworks.one_line: low-dimensional decoding of behaviorally relevant neural patterns under small-N constraints

core_question: Can a task-relevant autoencoder extract behaviorally-relevant low-dimensional neural representations from small fMRI datasets where standard models overfit?
key_assumptions: features of interest are those tied to subject behavior, not arbitrary variance
joint reconstruction-plus-classification objective regularizes representation learning
human fMRI per-subject samples are too few for standard deep models
manipulations: model architecture (TRACE vs standard autoencoder vs alternatives)
training set size severely truncated to mimic per-subject fMRI
domain (benchmark ML datasets vs fMRI of animals vs objects viewing)
key_findings: TRACE outperformed alternative models nearly unilaterally on truncated benchmarks
up to 12% increased classification accuracy over baselines
up to 56% improvement in recovering cleaner task-relevant representations
advantage held on fMRI from 59 subjects viewing animals and objects
interpretations: supervising the latent space with behavior yields cleaner neural representations under small-N
task-relevant autoencoding is a viable tool for human neuroimaging with limited samples
